content.assessment: This proposal is the closest structural analogue to the lens's own epistemic stance: it does not assume a single intervention can be evaluated in isolation and instead proposes infrastructure to track Hungary-specific causal attribution over time, directly addressing the lens's stated limitation that Portugal-style (or any) gains cannot be attributed to one isolated intervention. It is non-mandating and does not itself level quality or equity, but its stated purpose is to enable better-informed coordinated reform across whichever structural model is chosen, which matches the lens's emphasis on needing coordinated, evidence-based reform bundles rather than assuming any single lever works.
opportunities: Could generate the Hungary-specific causal evidence needed to determine which of the other structural proposals (T1-T5) actually work in this specific institutional context, directly serving the lens's causal-attribution concern
strengths: Explicitly designed to solve the causal-attribution problem central to this lens by tracking realized outcomes rather than assuming international evidence transfers directly to Hungary
Framework-level approach is compatible with, rather than competing against, structural reforms addressing quality and equity (church/state asymmetry, teacher shortage) documented elsewhere
threats: Historical pattern suggests political decisions have overridden technical evidence before, risking the framework being bypassed regardless of its analytical merit
weaknesses: An information/transparency framework alone does not itself level quality or equity; it is explicitly non-mandating and depends on other structural measures for actual impact
No direct evidence provided on whether a registry would counteract concentrated disadvantage (F-live-portuguese_reform-03) or the church/state selection asymmetry (F-live-portuguese_reform-08)
